top_topics.representative: The speakers argue trend following is a divergent strategy that profits by cutting losers and following winners across markets.
They think the Fed can cut 50 to 100 basis points over six to nine months if growth softens and recession risk rises.
He argues trend-following underperforms stocks partly because CTAs lack stock-only funds or meaningful equity exposure.
He argues stocks and bonds can correlate in stress, as seen in 2022, so permanent 60/40-style allocation can drag performance.
He claims diversification is the only free lunch, but the marginal benefit drops quickly; with pairwise correlation 0.5, 7–8 strategies nearly reaches the asymptote.
He says one purely systematic trader evolved from trend following to countertrend and then to mixed models as markets changed.
The strategy had only positive years since 2014 and 64% positive months, but its worst month was -14.5% in February 2020.
Momentum was applied to the aggregate U.S. market, trading only the direction of market momentum rather than stock deciles.
Binder argues the channel is expectations: mere fear that the central bank may cave raises inflation expectations now.
Price-range compression across markets reduced trend-following opportunities by shrinking available trend size over rolling three- and six-month windows.
He believes the AI winners today are chip sellers, firms with data, and connected infrastructure providers, while software beneficiaries remain unclear.
If markets have different Sharpe ratios, it can be optimal to abandon low-Sharpe markets even if they add diversification.
In March 2020, Treasury liquidity became far worse than volatility alone would predict, showing the market could not intermediate extreme liquidity demands.
The trade encourages poor habits because it wins about 90% of the time and tempts traders to add size into losses.
Managed futures are framed as a long-cycle diversifier: not a hedge every day, but a portfolio balancer across several market cycles.
Gold and some precious metals were bright spots, and energies also provided crisis offset for trend-followers.
He said the Fed’s extended QT runway matters because the stock of Fed holdings affects yield levels and helps keep long rates higher.
He argues successful quant methods start from a well-understood trading edge and are then systematized, not discovered by raw data mining.
Goldilocks soft landing is statistically rare; he cites 12 tightening cycles, eight recessions, and only four arguable soft landings since the 1960s.
The paper claims 87.5% of trades use exactly one lot when the spread is not constrained by one tick.
Post-crisis regulation made single-manager hedge funds harder to launch because operational, reporting, and risk burdens rose sharply.
Across minutes to decades, markets show different regimes: intraday mean reversion, 3-12 month trend persistence, and multi-year reversion or persistence flips.
New value stocks are younger firms with strong ROE, strong ROA, and high sales growth that became value after a stock-price drop.
Dever says the main advantage of systematic trading is disciplined risk management, preventing rationalized holding of losing positions.
Credit stress is turning, with default rates, bankruptcies, and delinquency rates on consumer debt all rising.
A 52% maximum drawdown is a major red flag because recovery from losing half the capital is very hard.
The unemployment rate has risen 60 bps from its low and is increasing about 30 bps every five months.
A long/short portfolio buying positive-news names and shorting negative-news names can outperform before costs, but costs materially reduce the edge.
He learned that buying options on every name in an early long basket reduced gap risk but became so expensive that the portfolio stopped making money.
Post-2009 funded long-vol funds relied mainly on implied-vol expansion and convexity from downside equity selloffs because that was the only meaningful tool available.
